narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 52% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just above the threshold we use to approve admissions. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.7, a very low LSAT score of 20, and your school rankings in the third decile for both first-year and third-year grades—meaning you performed below average compared to your peers, where typical students rank around the fifth or sixth decile. Your family income in the lower-middle bracket also factored in, as it's below the dataset average, along with being an Asian female planning full-time studies, though these are less common but not drastically outside norms. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your third-year school decile, which had the biggest pull toward predicting failure since it was lower than what succeeds for most students. Next in influence was your first-year decile, similarly dragging the prediction downward because it signals early academic struggles uncommon among bar passers. Your LSAT score ranked third in impact, standing out as much lower than the typical 37-point average that correlates with success. Family income followed, with lower-middle status pushing slightly toward risk compared to higher-earning families in the data. Your racial background as Asian had a modest effect toward failure in this context, though Asians make up only about 6% of successful cases. Undergraduate GPA and gender came next, both contributing to the risk due to being below benchmarks, while full-time status had the smallest role but still nudged against you. Overall, your academics—especially those deciles and LSAT—stood out as weaker than the strong profiles of students who typically pass the bar. To improve your odds next time, focus on boosting your academic performance through targeted study habits or additional coursework to raise future grade deciles, consider retaking the LSAT for a higher score since it's a key hurdle, and build bar-specific prep skills early, as these are changes within your control that could shift predictions favorably. You're close to the line at 52%, so persistent effort in these areas could make a real difference—keep pushing forward.
